speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 6.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I would like to add that about six months ago, just before Christmas I went to the first Holocaust Survivor Centre, which is in Nottingham. It took a non-Jewish family to come up with this. The two boys, Stephen Smith and his brother, they were on a kibbutz in Israel. They were so affected by what they saw and what they heard that they came back and told their parents about it. Their parents, they-- they went out themselves to Jerusalem, to Yad Vashem, and see what was going on. They decided to open up-- they have a large, old house. They turned it into a mini Yad Vashem. You would not believe the archives, and the photos, and this things they managed to get together. They also built on at the front of the house, an-- an-- a welcome place, where they meet you, and they greet you when you first arrive. I can't even begin to tell how we feel, the 45 Aid Society, about having a non-Jewish family to be the first one to open a Holocaust museum. And they've got for weeks booked schools and universities. It's all free to go in, and see this center. They take in trips even from our own group. Two of them are going in July, two loads, one from the 45 Aid Society and one from the Hendon Club, which I belong. It's a Holocaust Survivors Club, it's called. It's been opened about 18 months ago. And I'm very happy to go there. And we play cards, and we do this, and we do that. And it takes you-- somewhere to go out for a-- a day, an afternoon here and an afternoon there. But going back to the center, I-- we spent the whole day there. In fact, I was so taken by it, the coach was nearly leaving without me. I was going from reading, from photos, and from memories what I see in or saw in Yad Vashem itself. And I asked questions from Stephen, and every question I tried to trip him up. And every question he answer me correct-- he answered me which I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: For instance, at the beginning as you go in there, he had an ordinary photo of an ordinary kid as you go in. And I explained to him, why don't you put the photo of that little boy with his hands up with about three or four German soldiers around him? He said to me, I've got it. I normally change it around. I said, for the children who come from schools, when they open the door and they see that, they will remember that for many, many years. And that is a very good effect. He says, you're right. I will change it more often than not. And as I said, I asked-- tried to-- I didn't try to trip him up. I just said, why haven't you got this? He says, yes, I have. But I haven't got the room to show it all. And to me, it was-- I just can't get over it, the fact that it took a non-Jewish family on their own back. Also they had a little help from different archives, even from Yad Vashem in later stages. But when they started, it was all on their own back. And I think that was-- that's-- it's terrific, absolutely terrific.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'm almost at the end of my film, and I'm getting-- I've got my two sons with me. They're getting themselves [? fixed ?] up and ready-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --to come at-- at the-- for the last five minutes of my video. So as far as I'm concerned, I'll probably be sorry when you've gone. Probably remember, I didn't say this like I didn't remember about this Holocaust memorial. And what can I say? [SIGHS] Unfortunately, that's something that happens to everybody. They always say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --afterwards they remember other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I should have-- you know, they say, write down everything you want to bring up and sort of say. And, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. But the time comes, you don't. [LAUGHS] We're all the same. We're all the same. But if-- if my wife was here, she would have done it for me. And I would have done everything according to what she would have written down for me then. Because when I made my other tape, [INAUDIBLE] the same thing happened. I come away, saying, oh, I forgot to say this and I forgot to say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't actually say enough about my family in Israel, did I? Is it-- is still going, the tape? Well, going back to my family in Israel, if I may. I have a brother who has got two lovely sons. They've got-- be-- be-- between them they've got seven grandchildren. My brother has got seven grandchildren. I've got a-- his name is Chaim, and his wife's name is [? Rachel. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a sister. My older sister, which is called Fage. She came to Israel in 1951, with a boy and a girl. She lost her husband very young. Her son, David, married a Canadian girl. They are doing very well and they're frum. My other sister is named Etus. She has three children. Two are twins. They are a little bit more-- more modern. They-- their children's name is [? Aryeh, ?] [? Chana, ?] and their oldest's name is Chaia. She is named after my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my-- my-- I go and see them almost annually. Since first time I went, I realized that a lot of my-- our family went over before the war, luckily, to Is-- to Israel. I remember, we went to the end of the village to see them off. They went in horse and cart, like leaving-- like in the cowboys films. And through them, I found out that I've got a lot of cousins, and nephews, and nieces. And they came-- when I used to go at the beginning, don't you remember me? I lived on so and so. I lived on the corner of that road. I said, no, no, no. I don't remember you. But I remember your parents. Because we changed, and you changed. And it's very hard. I mean, do you remember me? I mean, when you're kids, and when you're suddenly 26, 36, of course you change a lot. And you don't remember people. But now, I mean, since I've been going, the first time I went to Israel was in '55, or maybe even '53. I think it was '55.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know all the people. When we-- when we have a wedding, I think last time they had a wedding, there was about 150 people, and were all close relations, not neighbors, just close relations. Because we had-- there was-- on the film, they made a video. And it was really lovely, really lovely to see. What I remember mostly about it, they have two little girls, my nephew. And one of them girls went up to each lady, presented them with a rose, and went up to each man and presented him with a button which said Welcome to Israel. And these girls, they welcomed us with a rhyme. Like for example, and to Uncle Steve and Auntie Julie, we welcome to-- and I can't do it. Everything had rhymed. And it was so terrific. This little one was only about five or six. It was really lovely. And I feel so at home. I tried to settle in Israel before. But being in the jewelry trade, it was very, very hard. My brothers would not-- would not say come or don't come, in case it don't work out. And after all, we did have three or four kids by the time I tried to. And eventually, I had made my life in England, and I've been here since 1945, which is almost a lifetime. And here I stayed. And so I've got somewhere to go to every year. I only have to pay my fare. And as I said before, and I've got holidays, going left, right, and center. But I did go last December, two weeks after my wife passed away. I was very, very low. I felt terrible. And I had a room to my own, and I found myself talking to her. I said, like, Julie, come and see the sunrise, or come see the sunset. Or wait till I get home and I'll tell Julie. I thought I was going round the bend. But I've learned since that life can take-- it's-- it's-- it's surprising what a human body, a human person can take. I mean with the camps I will say, I was too young to know what was happening. I was too young to realize that people were dying, left and right of me. I was too young to see that they were pulling out bodies, dead from the night before, from bunk beds. And it-- somehow or other it didn't-- it didn't sink in. It didn't hit me. Now, I go to just a friend's levaya, and it upsets me terribly. Because I don't know. Maybe I'm changing my ways. Maybe I'm older, and what they say, wiser. I don't know about that. And that's what happens. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you call the other two in, we'll finish this if we may. Sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it'd be a pleasure. I'd like to introduce you to my elder son, David. He-- he's sitting by the side of me. Laurie just left his work. It's Lawrence Pearl and David Pearl. They've come to help me out, to be in my film, which I'm very pleased that they made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- you have two daughters who aren't here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two daughters. One lives in a little village in Cheshire. She would have liked to have been here. But she couldn't. Then I've got another daughter-- that-- that's Helen, by the way. I've got another daughter, Kim Edith. She's got a little baby of two and a half, and unfortunately she caught a lot of-- she's got something. She-- at the moment they are in [? Rye ?] in a cottage. She would have loved to have come down today. But she's got-- her face come out in blotches. And she just couldn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very well at all. She couldn't see herself-- she wanted to come. I said, I said, Kim, leave it, leave it, leave it. Don't bring her out because it's too hot for you to come down two and a half hours, and go back two and a half hours, and all this. So I persuaded her not to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would-- would you like to say anything about your dad, and whether you feel that your life would have been any different if your father hadn't been a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He had a bad temper when we was younger. And I believe it was because of the effects of the war. And he had a little bit of treatment. And as he's got older, he's sort of matured. He's calmed down a lot. And he hasn't got-- hasn't got the temper that he used to have. But he did have a terrible temper when I was a young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It made me a little bit nervous, I think. But as he's mellowed out, [LAUGHS] he's been a lot--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Haven't ?] [? we all. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He's mellowed out, and much easier going now than he used to be. When I used to get hit, but he's-- he was never hit. You know, he's 10 years younger than me. [LAUGHS] And my older sister used to get hit as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ask him why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: Silly things, like if I was in-- if we would talk when we got sent to bed, if we were talking to each other instead of going to sleep, it could be for that. But you know, other times I probably deserved it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you think you were affected by your dad being a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Yeah. I think so. I-- I was the fortunate one because I was the youngest. So I'm the spoiled little son, who-- Dad was mellow by the time I was old enough to know what was happening, and-- but I do feel that it has affected my personality, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. I think when you're a young child, and you first find out that half your father's family had been wiped out, murdered. And you ask for what reason, and you're told because they were Jewish. You can either react in two ways, I think. You can either-- it depends on your personality in the first place. And you would either react by saying, well, that's going to make me more determined to state my Jewishness and be even more religious in that sense. Or otherwise and possibly the case with me, it might make you fearful of being different and fearful of religion. Because that is-- the-- from what you can see, the effect of people following a religion could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a fear of individuality, a fear of standing out in a crowd, I think that certainly when I was younger, it had a-- had a strong effect on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to add to David's-- what David said. What he didn't say is while they were running around upstairs, we had no carpets. I was working downstairs like a beaver. I couldn't concentrate, fine work I was doing. And I-- and we kept on shouting, get into your bed. Get in your bed. To no avail, until you got a hiding. And-- but today, if it happened today, with all these carpets and things, I probably wouldn't have heard him. But it wasn't because I was strict with them. It was the fact that below his bedroom I was trying to work. I was bringing work home. And I just couldn't get on with it, with their making the-- a noise, running to one bedroom to the other. [LAUGHS] Yeah. It's your turn. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you say? Is there anything else that you'd like to add? Either of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I would like you to talk a bit about your mother, you know. I mean how strict we were with you. You had to attend cheder. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was seven days a week, I had to get up and do something. Five days a week I was going to school. And the sixth day I was going to synagogue. And the seventh day I was going to Hebrew classes. And I used to begrudge that, knowing-- knowing that other kids in my school only got up five days a week to work. And I felt like too much was getting pushed onto me at once. I was bar mitzvahed at 13. But that still wasn't enough. I still had to carry on until I was 15. Because my mother wasn't Jewish, so I had to be confirmed as well into the Jewish faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right up until I was 15 years old, I-- you know, I felt like I was having to work hard at it, because of-- you know, being-- being Jewish. But I-- I-- I like being Jewish. I'm proud of being Jewish. And all-- all through my youth, I only had Jewish friends. I only mixed-- mixed with Jewish friends. It just felt-- felt more comfortable being with people who were Jewish. It didn't feel different, as my brother said.

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, when I mixed in with non-Jewish people, I-- I couldn't take any-- any jokes about me being Jewish or anything, you know, without--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --a fight. I had lots of fights. Almost every week I would have a fight, because I couldn't turn the other cheek. I took after my father, with the bad temper. I would have a go back at them. And even-- even if they knew that they could beat me in the end, they wouldn't bother. Because I know they got a hard time out of it. I'd give them a good hard time. And then other Jewish children used to come up to me and ask-- ask me to protect them. Because I'd-- I'd started to get a reputation for fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't go to Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Cheder only.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But even-- even-- if I may interrupt-- like David and Helen had to become Jewish in their own right. Because when-- at that time, my wife wasn't Jewish. But when Laurie and Kim came along, Julia had already become Jewish. I mean, he's 33. So she became Jewish-- and Kim is 38-- well before they were born. So she became Jewish, and they didn't have to go through the whole rigmarole about becoming Jewish. But he-- David and Helen did. And as I said, as soon as they started going properly to cheder and all that, she said, now I want to do it. Because I never pushed her. I never asked her. I left it to her own free will. And she said, now I want to. And she passed with flying colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you originally get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in originally in register office. But I've got married since in shul and under a chuppah and all that. And it was a-- a lovely thing. Many years later, I went down on my knees, and I said, Julie, will you marry me? I waited for another couple. They were in the same shoes as-- same position as me. We were going to have a double. And they kept on hemming and hawing, and hawing and hemming. And then they didn't go through with it. In fact, they're divorced now. I won't mention no names. I did. I got down on my knee. And I asked her. And she said, I'd love to. And we married. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the children were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, even-- and you know what? My daughter lived in America. And I didn't know until she walked in. You see, you took it out of me that she was-- she came. I said, this is the best thing anybody ever done to-- for me, to walk in. Which, I mean, I never dreamed that she would come all the way from America to our chuppah. Helen was there, and these two, and-- and Kim, and some [? in-laws-- ?] all there, all in shul. I had a lot of friends. It was short notice. But I think-- I think we done well. Don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lovely day. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lovely day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mum really appreciated it. She really enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lovely day, really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not-- that was two years ago. I've got the [HEBREW] there on the photo, which I would like you to take on film. As I said, she was ill. And I didn't do it because of that. Because we was talking for years about it. And it happened. And I'm very, very, very, very happy because of it. Very happy, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did-- we all loved our mother very much. And we still miss her very much, indeed. She was wonderful to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you could swing around to these-- I suppose you've got some of these photos from there, right around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll put them on afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, lovely. Yeah, and what can I say? Unless Laurie's got something else to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: About Mum, I don't really feel like words-- like words are enough to say what I feel about Mum. It's the devotion she showed to all of us and Dad throughout her life was beyond belief, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Incredible. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the type of woman who, even whilst very ill, couldn't keep out of the kitchen. I said, Julie, for 45 years you cooked for us the [? mush. ?] Do you mind if-- if you-- sit down. We will-- I'm making dinner. We will-- All right, then. She done you a great big favor. She couldn't keep out of the kitchen. She was always working. All my friends, a lot of my friends, they've got cleaners. They've got gardeners. I had a cleaner for about 6, 8 weeks towards the end before she died. She had done the cleaning, the gardening. She worked-- she worked two or three days a week for-- in shops. We had our own shop. She worked in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just can't describe her. There's no words. As Laurie said, there's no words to describe that woman. She was just one in a million. And I mean it, one in a million. She was-- you know what they say-- the-- the-- what does this say, the song go? The-- the-- the [INAUDIBLE] and the upstairs, and then how does that song go? She was all of that. And we miss her very much, and we still have a good little cry. And-- but we do meet twice a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kim comes with the baby. We have Friday night meal together. And Wednesday Laurie comes and David is here, and we have a dinner. I'm capable of cooking for them, and all that. And so are they-- they-- they can as well. And we keep-- we keep together. We keep close together.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's really nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, good. And although, you know, as our friend, you know, you only have got to mention the name Julie, and [SOB] that's it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us who's in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this picture is of my father, David Perl. I believe it's about '42, '43 it was taken, 1942, 1943. Unfortunately he didn't make it. He died in-- in Germany. I don't know which camp, from 25 beatings, 25 lashes he got, and he never recovered. And I miss him very much. I always have. In fact, as I mentioned on the video before, I felt terrible when I was his age and I was alive and he died. And he was not alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I treasure this picture very much. I treasure this picture very much. It's me and my wife-to-be. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It was on our engagement. This is our engagement picture, I believe. Well, believe-- I know it is. And if I had only known what a happy, happy life I would have, I would have jumped up to the moon. And she was a lovely woman, a lovely wife, and-- and a lovely person to be with. Unfortunately she's passed away, as I already said.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As you can see, this is a photo of my family. And unfortunately, Helen is missing, because she was living in South Africa at the time. David is in the front, then is my wife Julie, then me, and then Kim, and then the bar mitzvah boy himself, Laurie. We had a very, very happy family life. You can see by the photos, there's no falsehood about that at home. And I'm proud to have been their father, to be their father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my older daughter, Helen, which I named after my mother. Her name is Chaia in-- in Ivrit, or in Yiddish. And that's her husband, [? Graham. ?] They got married in 1973, I believe. But I'm not too sure about it. They were only-- there-- she was 18, and he was about 20. I think they're very happy. They've been all over the world. We haven't seen much of them. And she's a lovely girl, very homely, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is my daughter Kim and her husband, Bruce. They have been married about 11 years. They've got a very nice life together. He's a little bit older than she is. But she's a lovely person, good at-- good heart. [BACKGROUND NOISE] She would give you her last penny if you needed it. She worked originally on the TV advertising and all that. But now she's got a lovely little baby, and she has given up work. And I think they're very, very happy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: My oldest-- Yeah, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: --my older son, David, was named after my father. He's a very good-hearted boy. But he's been unlucky in his life. This is a photo of his younger son, Laurie, named [? Lazar, ?] at Laurie's bar mitzvah. He's had his ups and downs, but he's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger son, Laurie. Real name [? Lazar. ?] He's named after my grandfather. And that's his wedding photo. He's been married about three years to a nice girl, [? Claire. ?] They're very happy. They both work very hard, and they play hard. Very nice boy, never had any trouble with him whatsoever, nice kid, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this one is-- is Nicky, the first granddaughter. She is going to be 21 in September. She's a lovely kid. She's very, very nice. First time I seen her when she was about 27 months. She was born in Cape Town. And lovely little blond and blue-eyed. She's good at heart, nice kid. And she's also holding my youngest granddaughter, which happens to be her-- her cousin, Harley.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my second granddaughter. Her name is Kersten. She's a bit of a rebel. Her mother has got a lot of trouble with her. But she's OK. She's taking at the moment some exams. And I don't see enough of them, really. They're very nice children. They wouldn't dream of saying anything to me to upset me. And they always-- always-- when I ring, they always say, hello, Granddad. When are you coming down? And all the rest of it. A pretty nice girl, but still a rebel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] This is my third granddaughter. Unfortunately, I haven't got any grandsons yet. Her name is Harley. She-- her mother is doting on her. That's Kim. She had her a little-- at 37 years of age. And she thinks the world of her. And she's a lovely, lovely little girl, really lovely. She comes on the phone, and she says to me, Papa. And my heart melts. Papa.
